caveats: a: No six-digit task mix was found.
rho: Implementation varies by hardware, software content, and safety criticality.
e: The assigned 157-firm count is margin-bridged.
s5: Technology or product-line acquisitions are not necessarily control transfers.
q: Aftermarket and proprietary products retain more than build-to-print programs.
d5: Technology adoption is not a direct six-digit shipment forecast.
o: Integration can reduce discrete units without eliminating hardware manufacturing.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Motor Vehicle Electrical and Electronic Equipment Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat per-unit supply under OEM programs, qualified contracts, distributor replenishment, and aftermarket orders.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive OEM plants, software-only firms, distributors without manufacturing, shell brands, and financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent LMM manufacturers of vehicle electrical, electronic, power-control, lighting, sensing, wiring, and related equipment for repeat external OEM and aftermarket customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures broader motor-vehicle parts, where assemblers are 23.77% and metal workers 18.41%, while management, engineering, computer, office, and quality roles also appear. I assign AI exposure to design support, requirements, testing analysis, documentation, quoting, planning, and customer service, not physical assembly, validation, inspection, or handling. Electronics suppliers likely have more technical information work than the four-digit average, so the proxy may understate exposure.
caveats: No six-digit task mix was found.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory at 20-30 independent automotive electronics plants would change the range.
a.rationale: Technical documentation and analysis broaden exposure, but production and safety validation remain embodied.

d5.bridge: DOE identifies inverters, DC/DC converters, chargers, motors, wiring, and thermal controls as essential EV components; NHTSA states ADAS depends on electronics, sensors, and computing. I translate EV adoption and rising electronic content per vehicle into positive demand for the broad current basket. This may overstate LMM domestic output if integration, imports, or semiconductor suppliers capture value and understate legacy electrical attrition.
caveats: Technology adoption is not a direct six-digit shipment forecast.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year OEM sourcing volumes by wiring, sensors, controllers, lighting, and power electronics would change the range.
d5.rationale: Electrification, ADAS, connectivity, and power management increase content, offset by integration, platform cycles, and price erosion.

e.change_evidence: A firm census linking ownership, manufacturing content, recurring revenue, and normalized EBITDA would change eligibility.
e.rationale: Independent equipment suppliers normally have repeat external programs; captive sites, software-only companies, import brands, and out-of-band firms are ineligible.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $4,588.7M / sectoral output (T30) $27,181.4M, year 2023

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $468,562 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 336320); class <5: 141 firms, avg revenue/firm $583,248 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $52,726 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 75 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,828,507 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $165,297 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 65 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,665,954 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $331,402 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 108 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,281,213 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,200,622 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $62,714,163 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,669,360 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 63 firms, avg revenue/firm $356,119,794 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $32,193,229 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 157

caveats: Integration can reduce discrete units without eliminating hardware manufacturing.
o.change_evidence: OEM architecture roadmaps for centralized computing, wireless replacement, integration, and insourcing would change o.
o.rationale: Remaining hardware demand requires accountable design, fabrication, assembly, validation, and supply. Software can consolidate functions but cannot replace physical sensors, conductors, and power electronics.

caveats: Aftermarket and proprietary products retain more than build-to-print programs.
q.change_evidence: OEM contracts showing productivity sharing, rebates, cost-downs, tooling, and software-maintenance terms would change retention.
q.rationale: Qualified designs and switching costs support retention, while OEM cost-downs, open-book terms, warranty exposure, redesign, and global competition pass savings through.

caveats: Implementation varies by hardware, software content, and safety criticality.
rho.change_evidence: Audited hours, defects, validation outcomes, and launch timing from supplier AI deployments would change the estimate.
rho.rationale: AI can support requirements, code and document review, testing analysis, planning, and support, but functional safety, cybersecurity, OEM validation, traceability, and human release constrain implementation.

caveats: Technology or product-line acquisitions are not necessarily control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Capital IQ, and supplier ownership changes reconciled to the eligible list would change the range.
s5.rationale: Qualifications, designs, customer platforms, and technical teams are transferable, but no eligible-firm control-transfer denominator exists.

principal_driver: Rising electrical and electronic content per vehicle.
principal_weakness: OEM bargaining and safety-validation constraints limit realized and retained savings.
